text:
Paris 2024 organisers have been hit with scathing criticism after Belgium announced its withdrawal from the mixed triathlon as an athlete was reportedly hospitalised from swimming in the River Seine.

The Paris 2024 Olympic Games has faced a nightmare scenario as a triathlete has reportedly been hospitalised by an E. Coli infection caught while swimming in the River Seine.

The Belgium Olympic team has officially withdrawn from the mixed relay triathlon on Monday (local time) after female athlete Claire Michel fell seriously ill.

Belgian newspaper De Standaard has reported: “Claire Michel has been ill for four days and had to go to the hospital for treatment.”

“She was infected with E. Coli bacteria, an examination revealed. That bacterium can cause gastrointestinal problems. The cause may be the polluted Seine.”

The news has devastated the nation’s gold medal aspirations as the Belgian Olympic and Interfederal Committee (BOIC) announced the team’s withdrawal on Monday.

“BOIC and Belgian Triathlon must unfortunately announce that the ‘Belgian Hammers’ will not be starting the mixed relay at the Paris Olympic Games,” the statement said.

“The BOIC and Belgian Triathlon hope that lessons will be learned for the next triathlon competitions at the Olympic Games.”

The River Seine has been at the centre of ongoing controversy surrounding the Paris Olympic Games for several months.

France had reportedly spent almost $2.5 billion on cleaning the river for the Olympics, causingParisiennes to threaten a poo protestover perceived excessive expense.

Despite the investment, the Men’sTriathlon had to be postponed last Tuesday, four hours before the scheduled start time, due to unsafe levels of bacteria.

While the river was sufficiently clean by Wednesday, Belgian athlete Jolien Vermeylen said she felt debris in the water while swimming.

“While swimming under the bridge, I felt and saw things that we shouldn’t think about too much,” Vermeylen told Belgian media.

“We’ll know tomorrow if I’m sick or not… It doesn’t taste like Coca-Cola or Sprite, of course,” she said last week.

The Paris Olympics has marked the first time swimmers have plunged into the Seine as a swimming ban has been in place since 1923 due to water pollution.

Officials in Paris now hold hope that no further illnesses or withdrawals will disrupt the upcoming mixed relay.

The Australian Triathlon team said its members were “all healthy” following individual races as they hope to defeat the heavy favourites, France, at 4pm on Monday.
